title: Rhubarb and ginger cake
subtitle: A soft traybake with seasonal rhubarb and two forms of ginger
excerpt: A simple traybake that uses rhubarb and both ground and stem ginger. The rhubarb sinks into the batter during baking and keeps the crumb moist; the stem ginger syrup, stirred into the mix, adds a mellow warmth underneath the sharper ground ginger note.
sourceNotes: Rhubarb traybakes with ginger are a well-established combination in British home baking. This recipe synthesises elements of standard British traybake technique with the classic rhubarb-ginger pairing.

recipe.yieldDescription: One 20 cm by 30 cm traybake (12 to 16 squares)
recipe.freezeNotes: Cut into squares, wrap individually, and freeze. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps well at room temperature for 3 days. The rhubarb flavour deepens after the first day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 160 degrees C; check at 35 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: Stem ginger is young ginger root preserved in sugar syrup, sold in jars. In baking, both the ginger pieces themselves (chopped) and the syrup they are stored in are useful: the syrup adds concentrated ginger flavour and keeps the crumb moist for longer than a cake baked without it. The heat of stem ginger is gentler and more rounded than fresh ginger, and it combines well with sharper ground ginger.

content.text: This is a cake that depends on having rhubarb in season. Forced rhubarb from January to April gives a sweeter, pinkier result; garden rhubarb from spring onward is more tart and a deeper red. Both work well. Using two forms of ginger gives the cake more range: ground ginger provides the initial warmth, and
adds moisture and a longer, mellower finish. The rhubarb is laid in pieces over the batter rather than stirred in; it sinks as the cake bakes.

Preheat the oven to 180 degrees C (conventional). Grease and line the tin. Whisk together the flour, baking powder, bicarbonate of soda, ground ginger, cinnamon, and salt in a large bowl. In a separate bowl, whisk together the brown sugar, melted butter, eggs, milk, chopped stem ginger, and stem ginger syrup. Pour the wet mixture into the dry and stir until just combined; a few small lumps are fine. Tip into the prepared tin and spread level. Lay the rhubarb pieces over the top in a single layer and scatter the demerara sugar over. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes, until a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean. The rhubarb will be tender and slightly caramelised. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes, then cut into squares.
